authoritative_sources.title: Fedwire Funds Service
authoritative_sources.publisher: Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve System
authoritative_sources.source_class: Public servicing, payments, or policy workflow rules
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: The Federal Reserve operates the Fedwire Funds Service and is the authoritative public source for its timing, participant roles, and settlement finality characteristics.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: wire-transfer intake, release, and execution timing review

authoritative_sources.title: Operating Circular 6: Funds Transfers Through the Fedwire Funds Service
authoritative_sources.publisher: Federal Reserve Banks / Federal Reserve Financial Services
authoritative_sources.source_class: Public servicing, payments, or policy workflow rules
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: Operating Circular 6 establishes the public operating duties and responsibilities for senders and recipients of Fedwire funds transfers.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: payment execution controls and message-handling review

authoritative_sources.title: Operating Circular 7: Fedwire Securities Service
authoritative_sources.publisher: Federal Reserve Banks / Federal Reserve Financial Services
authoritative_sources.source_class: Public servicing, payments, or policy workflow rules
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: Operating Circular 7 is the authoritative public rule set for Fedwire securities accounts, book-entry transfers, and participant obligations in securities and custody movement handling.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: securities-settlement and custody movement evidence review

authoritative_sources.title: FFIEC BSA/AML Manual - Funds Transfers Recordkeeping
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: FFIEC provides the interagency examination guidance used by U.S. banking regulators for transfer recordkeeping, Travel Rule handling, and examination of funds-transfer controls.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: payment record validation and AML-sensitive transfer review

authoritative_sources.title: A Framework for OFAC Compliance Commitments
authoritative_sources.publisher: U.S. Department of the Treasury, Office of Foreign Assets Control
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: OFAC is the primary U.S. sanctions authority, and its framework is the direct public baseline for sanctions-compliance expectations that constrain payment execution and exception handling.

authoritative_sources.title: 12 CFR Part 1005 (Regulation E) - Remittance Transfer Disclosures and Error Resolution
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: CFPB is the authoritative U.S. consumer-protection regulator for Regulation E, which governs remittance disclosures, cancellation rights, and error-resolution handling when treasury operations touch consumer transfers.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: consumer-impact payment exception and remediation routing

authoritative_sources.title: Principles for Financial Market Infrastructures
authoritative_sources.publisher: Committee on Payments and Market Infrastructures and IOSCO / Bank for International Settlements
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: CPMI-IOSCO PFMI is the recognized global public control standard for settlement finality, custody, operational risk, reconciliation, and resilient financial-market-infrastructure operations.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: reconciliation, settlement finality, and control design review

workflow_stages.name: payment and settlement intake validation
workflow_stages.description: Confirm that the transaction, customer, product, account, approval, and cutoff prerequisites required to work the queue item are present before execution progresses.
source_classes: Public servicing, payments, or policy workflow rules

workflow_stages.name: execution, queue progression, and cutoff handling
workflow_stages.description: Advance, hold, or rework payments and settlement items based on approvals, release controls, timing windows, and authoritative transaction records.
source_classes: Public servicing, payments, or policy workflow rules

workflow_stages.name: custody, securities movement, and completion evidence capture
workflow_stages.description: Validate book-entry movement, custody-account updates, and completion evidence before treating a securities or treasury movement as fulfilled.
source_classes: Public servicing, payments, or policy workflow rules

workflow_stages.name: reconciliation and exception queue handling
workflow_stages.description: Use ledgers, nostro statements, sub-ledgers, and settlement confirmations to investigate breaks and determine whether items should remain open, rework, or escalate.

workflow_stages.name: fraud, aml, sanctions, and consumer-impact escalation
workflow_stages.description: Stop work and route to the correct specialist when sanctions, suspicious activity, fraud indicators, or consumer-protection obligations change the outcome.

artifacts: payment instructions and wire message content
dual-control approval history and callback-verification artifacts
cash position reports and intraday liquidity views
nostro statements, general-ledger balances, and treasury sub-ledgers
Fedwire funds confirmations and securities book-entry confirmations
custody-account positions, settlement-status messages, and fail notices
exception queues, investigation notes, and break comments
fraud, AML, sanctions, and customer-impact case artifacts
disclosures, notices, and communication logs when consumer-impact handling is in scope
systems_of_record: core banking or cash ledger platform
Fedwire funds and securities interfaces or correspondent-bank systems
fraud-monitoring, sanctions-screening, and AML case-management systems

allowed: Classify work as in-scope, out-of-scope, or blocked on missing prerequisites.
Request the minimum tenant facts, authoritative records, and product or jurisdiction context needed to continue safely.
Advance, hold, rework, or complete work based on documented lifecycle state and visible evidence.
Prepare evidence-backed status updates, completion records, exception summaries, and escalation notes.
Route work to the correct approver or adjacent specialist when ownership shifts.
escalate: Fraud, AML, sanctions, or suspicious-activity indicators change the outcome.
Consumer-protection obligations, disclosures, or error-resolution rights may apply.
Policy conflict, jurisdiction conflict, or tenant-specific approval maps materially change the answer.
Settlement, custody, or reconciliation records conflict and require human decisioning.
refuse: The request requires formal fraud or AML disposition, sanctions clearance, legal interpretation, investment advice, or financial approval authority.
Required records, approvals, or tenant control procedures are missing and would change the result.
The request asks the lane to mark work complete without authoritative completion evidence.

domain_failure_modes: Unsupported payment release or post-cutoff processing without required approvals
Marking payment or securities movement complete when authoritative system evidence is missing
Ledger, nostro, or custody break resolution that ignores unresolved discrepancies
Failure to escalate suspicious, sanctioned, or fraud-sensitive transactions
Consumer-impact transfer handling that ignores disclosure or error-resolution obligations
Cross-business-unit generalization of approval or callback rules without tenant evidence
Overstating operational handling as legal, compliance, or treasury strategy authority

scenario_family_coverage.scenario_family: Disclosure or reconciliation conflict that requires escalation
Execution exception that requires rework or escalation
Request to mark work complete without evidence of completion

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: Non-U.S. settlement rails and custodian-specific workflow rules
unresolved_ambiguities.details: The public baseline is U.S.-centric and centers on Federal Reserve, FFIEC, OFAC, and CFPB sources. Tenant retrieval is required where non-Fed settlement rails, offshore custodians, or non-U.S. remittance obligations materially change the workflow.
